name: Blacks Fork Creek
short_description: BLM public land along Blacks Fork Creek offering free rockhounding for agate, jasper, and quartz specimens. High elevation creek environment with excellent collecting potential year-round for exper...
description:
Blacks Fork Creek sits in the Uinta County region of southwestern Wyoming at 6,302 feet elevation, offering rockhounders access to excellent collecting opportunities on Bureau of Land Management (BLM) public land. This pristine creek environment provides a mix of wash deposits and hillside outcrops where agates, jasper varieties, and quartz specimens weather from surrounding formations. The area benefits from minimal development pressure and excellent access without fees or permits, making it ideal for serious rockhounders seeking quality material in a remote mountain setting.

The geology here reflects Wyoming's complex Precambrian basement rocks and Paleozoic sedimentary sequences. Blacks Fork Creek drainage has exposed silica-rich formations through millions of years of erosion, concentrating colorful agate nodules and jasper specimens in creek gravels and along cutbanks. The creek's high elevation and mountain setting mean reliable water flow and access to both wet and dry collecting zones depending on season. Many specimens here display the rich earth tones and banding patterns characteristic of northern Rocky Mountain agate deposits.

Collecting along Blacks Fork Creek rewards explorers willing to work creek beds and examine hillside talus slopes. Fresh specimens frequently weather from exposed ledges, while older deposits concentrate in ancient terraces above current water level. The remote location means less competition from other collectors and pristine undisturbed material. Early morning hikes yield the best results, as light angles reveal translucent qualities in specimens that blend with surrounding rock.

Because this site requires some hiking and remote travel, it suits intermediate to advanced rockhounders comfortable navigating mountain terrain and assessing geological features. The combination of free public access, quality specimens, and wilderness setting makes Blacks Fork Creek a destination for serious collectors willing to invest effort in off-trail prospecting.
history: This region sits within the Wyoming Basin and Range geological province where Precambrian metamorphic rocks and Paleozoic sedimentary sequences were uplifted and exposed during the Laramide Orogeny. Blacks Fork Creek's canyon walls expose these ancient formations, and millions of years of stream erosion have concentrated silica-rich agate and jasper nodules in the creek gravels and alongside weathered outcrops.
tips: Search the active creek bed after spring runoff recedes (June onward) when fresh material has been deposited and older specimens remain accessible on exposed banks. Look for color variation in creek gravels - agates and jasper often stand out as translucent or waxy-surfaced stones distinct from surrounding dull matrix rocks. Hike the creek terraces above current water level where ancient deposits have concentrated specimens; examine cutbanks carefully for partially exposed nodules protruding from hillsides. Bring a small loupe to check for banding and translucency before hauling material, and pack a 5-gallon bucket to maximize your daily 25-pound limit while staying within FLPMA regulations.
terrain_notes: High elevation mountain creek environment at 6,302 feet. Terrain includes active creek channel with rocky footing, loose cobbles, and occasional swift water sections during runoff. Surrounding hillsides slope steeply from creek bed with mixed sagebrush and conifer vegetation. Creek banks feature exposed cutbanks up to 8-10 feet high with active erosion exposing fresh material. Walking distances from likely parking areas range 0.5 to 2+ miles through foothills and along water courses. Footing is rocky and uneven throughout; sturdy boots with ankle support strongly recommended.
best_season: June - September. Spring runoff (May-June) freshens creek deposits, while summer months provide safe water conditions and accessible terrain. Fall (September) remains productive but weather becomes unpredictable. Winter snow at this elevation restricts access November through May.
written_directions: From Evanston, take US 80 east for approximately 18 miles. Turn south onto Forest Service Road 086 (or local county road serving the Blacks Fork drainage). Follow this road for approximately 5-8 miles as it climbs into the mountains, watching for creek-side pullouts. Park at any safe turnout near creek crossings where you can see exposed banks or stream gravels. GPS coordinates 41.568326, -110.141358 mark productive section; adjust parking based on seasonal water levels and road conditions. High clearance vehicle recommended; check with Evanston ranger district for current road status before visiting.
road_conditions: Forest Service dirt road with possible washboard surface. High clearance helpful. Road may be impassable during heavy rain or snow; confirm current status with BLM office.
parking_notes: Limited pullouts along creek access road. Park off-road where vehicle is safe and does not obstruct access. No developed parking area; plan for 2-4 vehicle capacity at most informal turnouts.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA. No commercial collection. No motorized excavation equipment. No heavy machinery. Respect private property boundaries in mixed ownership areas. Leave area cleaner than you found it. All specimens must be hand-collected using hand tools only.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation or heavy machinery
Hand tools only
Respect private property
Pack out all trash
nearest_services: Evanston, WY - approximately 20 miles north via US 80. Full services including gas, food, water, and lodging available. No services at or near collecting site.
accessibility_notes: Uneven rocky creek bed terrain with loose cobbles, slippery when wet, and swift current sections during runoff. Hillside slopes are steep with loose scree and vegetation obstacles. No wheelchair access. Not suitable for visitors with limited mobility, young children without adult supervision, or those uncomfortable with remote mountain hiking. Sturdy waterproof boots and trekking poles strongly recommended.

faq.question: What agates and jasper can you find at Blacks Fork Creek in Wyoming?
faq.answer: Blacks Fork Creek yields colorful agate nodules with banding patterns typical of northern Rocky Mountain deposits, along with multiple jasper varieties in earth tones of brown, red, and cream. Chalcedony specimens and clear to milky quartz are also common. Specimens range from thumbnail size to fist-sized pieces in creek gravels and exposed hillside deposits.

faq.question: Is rockhounding free at Blacks Fork Creek or do you need a permit?
faq.answer: Rockhounding at Blacks Fork Creek is completely free. The site is on BLM public land requiring no permit for personal use collection. You are limited to 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations, and all collection must be by hand tools only - no motorized equipment allowed.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to rockhound Blacks Fork Creek?
faq.answer: June through September is optimal. Spring runoff in May-June freshens creek deposits with new material, while summer months provide safe water conditions and accessible terrain. Fall (September) remains productive but weather becomes unpredictable. Winter snow restricts access from November through May at this 6,302-foot elevation.

faq.question: How do you reach Blacks Fork Creek from Evanston Wyoming?
faq.answer: From Evanston, take US 80 east for approximately 18 miles, then turn south onto Forest Service Road 086. Follow this road 5-8 miles into the mountains, watching for creek-side pullouts near 41.568326, -110.141358. A high-clearance or all-wheel-drive vehicle is recommended. Confirm road status with the local ranger district before visiting.

faq.question: What tools do you need for rockhounding at Blacks Fork Creek?
faq.answer: Bring hand tools only - a small chisel, rock hammer, and pry bar work well for extracting specimens from cutbanks and creek beds. A small loupe helps evaluate quality before collecting. A 5-gallon bucket or backpack helps you maximize your 25-pound daily limit. Bring sturdy waterproof boots, trekking poles for stability on loose rock, and plenty of water since this is a remote site with no services.

faq.question: Is Blacks Fork Creek rockhounding suitable for beginners?
faq.answer: Blacks Fork Creek suits intermediate to advanced rockhounders comfortable with remote mountain hiking, uneven rocky terrain, and creek crossing during higher water levels. The remoteness (4/5 rating), 6,300+ foot elevation, and steep hillsides make it challenging for beginners. Families with older children (12+) and experienced hikers will have better success. Start with closer, easier BLM sites first if you are new to rockhounding.

meta_description: Free BLM rockhounding for agate, jasper, and quartz at Blacks Fork Creek, Wyoming. No permit needed. Maximum 25 lbs per day. High elevation mountain creek access.
